name: Physical Quantities
description: Things you can measure in physics—length, time, mass, speed, force, energy.

content.overview: "I'm tall" says nothing precise — tall compared to what? "I'm 180 cm tall" says everything, because it pairs a number with a unit. That pairing is what a physical quantity is: a measurable value that always needs both a number and a unit. {{viz:diagram-01}}
content.explanation:
A physical quantity is anything you can attach a number and a unit to. "I'm tall" is too vague. "I'm 180 cm tall" works — number (180) and unit (cm).

Every physical quantity needs both parts:
- **Number**: how much
- **Unit**: in what

Examples:
- Length: 5 meters
- Mass: 2 kilograms
- Time: 10 seconds
- Speed: 60 kilometers per hour {{viz:chart-01}}

Physical quantities split into two categories.

**Scalars** — just a number, no direction. Mass (10 kg), temperature (25°C).

**Vectors** — a number plus a direction. Velocity (10 m/s north), force (50 N upward). {{viz:diagram-02}}

The direction matters: "10 m/s" and "10 m/s east" are different statements, and "100 N" is not the same as "100 N downward." Drop the direction from a vector and you've dropped half the information. {{viz:anim-01}}
keyPoints: A physical quantity = a number + a unit
Always include the unit (meters, seconds, kg, etc.)
Scalars have just a number (mass, temperature, time)
Vectors have a number and a direction (velocity, force, acceleration)
content.whyItMatters: Physical quantities are the vocabulary of physics — nothing precise can be said without them. Predicting where a ball lands or how fast a car needs to brake requires the right quantities, measured correctly, with the right units. Without that, it's just a guess.
